title: The For Loop 🔄
description: Iterate over a sequence or range of numbers.

slides.title: Ranges 📏
slides.content:
Before we look at the For loop, we need to understand a very cool Ruby feature: The **Range**.

A range represents a sequence from a start value to an end value.

You create a range by placing two dots `..` between two numbers.

`1..10` represents the numbers 1 through 10.

slides.title: The For Loop
slides.content:
A `for` loop allows you to grab every individual item out of a sequence (like a range) one by one.

```ruby
for number in 1..5
  puts "Currently on number: #{number}"
end
```

It grabs `1`, runs the code, then grabs `2`, runs the code... etc.

slides.title: For Loop in Action
slides.content: Notice how the temporary variable `i` changes its value every single time the loop cycles.
slides.codeSnippet:
puts "Counting to 3:"
for i in 1..3
  puts i
end

slides.title: Quick Check 🧠
slides.content: How do you capture the value in a for loop?

quizData.question: In the syntax `for dog in 1..5`, what is `dog`?

options.text: A temporary variable that holds the current iteration value (1, then 2, then 3...).

quizData.explanation: You can name this variable anything you want! It will temporarily hold whichever value the loop is currently highlighting.

slides.title: Two vs Three Dots 🎾
slides.content:
You can make ranges using two dots `..` OR three dots `...`.

The difference is critical:
* `1..5` is inclusive (1, 2, 3, 4, 5).
* `1...5` is EXCLUSIVE (1, 2, 3, 4). 

It goes *up to* the last number, but doesn't include it.

quizData.question: What is the very last number printed by `for x in 10...20`?
quizData.explanation: Because it uses three dots `...`, it stops *just before* 20.

slides.title: Rubyists Don't Love For
slides.content: While `for` loops exist in Ruby, they are actually very rarely used by professional Ruby developers. They vastly prefer our next topic: the `.each` iterator.
